unicode.value: Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 2

Todas las imágenes incluidas en esta unidad han sido generadas mediante inteligencia artificial a través de la herramienta ChatGPT (OpenAI). Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 2

Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 2

Introduction to the Session

Mistakes are a natural part of any workplace, but how you communicate about them can make a big difference. Whether it’s admitting an error, offering an apology, or outlining a solution, using the right language helps you stay professional and build trust with colleagues and clients.

In this session, you’ll watch a realistic workplace conversation from a video titled “Mistakes at Work.” The dialogue features authentic English used in professional settings, focusing on how people address errors and propose solutions. This session is designed to enhance your listening skills and equip you with practical language for workplace challenges.

🎧 What You’ll Learn

This session will help you develop key professional competencies through targeted listening and language practice. You’ll focus on:

  • Listening for specific information: Identify key details and expressions used to describe problems and solutions.
  • Understanding tone and intention: Notice how speakers’ emotions (e.g., regret, confidence) and purposes (e.g., apologizing, reassuring) shape their language.
  • Using practical vocabulary: Practice phrasal verbs (e.g., “deal with,” “sort out”) and functional expressions (e.g., “We’re working on it”) to handle workplace issues.
  • Responding professionally: Learn natural, polite phrases to communicate effectively in emails, calls, or meetings.

💡 Tip: Pay attention to how speakers use tone to convey empathy or urgency. This will help you respond appropriately in similar situations.

Listening Strategies – Before Watching the Video

How to Approach the Listening Task

In this session, you will watch the video “Mistakes at Work” from BBC Learning English. The video is realistic and includes natural expressions, spoken interaction, and useful phrases for the workplace. To help you understand it more easily, try using these strategies — based on the CEFR recommendations for B1+ learners:

  • Use your knowledge of the context. You know this is about making mistakes at work. Think of typical words you might hear: error, issue, apologise, fix.
  • Focus on the speaker’s intention, not every word. What is the speaker trying to do — admit a mistake, apologise, explain a solution?
  • Notice how people feel. Pay attention to tone, hesitation, or emotional words. This helps you understand attitudes and relationships.
  • Listen for keywords and repeated phrases. Common phrases like “I’m sorry that...” or “We’ve taken steps to...” will help you follow the ideas.
  • Use the activity structure to guide your listening. The video is divided into clear parts — focus only on one section at a time.
  • Listen more than once. First, get the general idea. Then, listen again to catch details and answer the questions.

Listening is not just about hearing — it’s about understanding the situation, the purpose, and the message. Use these strategies to get the most out of this session.

🎧 Listening Practice – Mistakes at Work

📺 Watch First

Before you start the activities, watch the video “Office English – Mistakes at Work” from BBC Learning English.



📝 The activities below are divided by sections of the video to help you focus on each part and understand the key expressions more easily.

✅ Activity 1 – Understanding the Problem (0:00–1:40)

Instructions: Listen to the first part of the video and choose the correct answer.

1. What feeling is mentioned at the beginning when receiving a message that says “Hi”?




2. According to the speaker, what is important when making mistakes?




✅ Activity 2 – Useful Phrases (2:00–3:35)

Instructions: Listen and complete the phrases using the correct missing word.

1. There’s an

2. I’ve realised that my team made an

3. I’ve accidentally sent the email out early, but I have a

✅ Activity 3 – Apologising and Responding (4:10–5:50)

Instructions: Match each phrase with what it does.

1. I’m really sorry about that.

2. Let me investigate the problem.

3. I’m sorry that you’re upset.

4. I’m sorry if you’re upset.

✅ Activity 4 – Solutions and Prevention (6:10–10:00)

Instructions: Listen and choose the correct answer.

1. What does “rest assured” mean in this context?




2. What is a “debrief”?

Vocabulary and Practice – Responding to Problems at Work

Why This Vocabulary Matters

When a problem arises at work — a missed deadline, a technical glitch, or an upset client — your ability to respond clearly and professionally makes all the difference. This section equips you with practical vocabulary to describe issues, propose solutions, and reassure colleagues or clients, ensuring you sound confident and competent.

You’ll focus on two key areas: phrasal verbs for taking action and functional expressions for empathetic and professional communication. These tools are designed for real-world workplace scenarios, such as emails, phone calls, or team meetings.

  • Explain problems clearly and concisely
  • Collaborate with colleagues to resolve issues
  • Communicate politely with clients or stakeholders
  • Build confidence in handling workplace challenges

Vocabulary Focus – Dealing with Problems at Work

Why This Vocabulary Matters

Navigating workplace challenges — like delays, complaints, or technical issues — requires clear and professional communication. This vocabulary equips you with practical expressions and phrasal verbs to address problems confidently, whether in meetings, emails, or customer interactions.

In this session, you’ll learn two key sets of language tools: phrasal verbs for problem-solving and functional expressions for supportive communication.

🎧 Complementary Audio – Guided Explanation
This audio supports you as you go through the lesson. Listen to it for a step-by-step explanation with spoken examples. It’s especially helpful if you prefer learning by listening or want to review while doing other tasks.

🔧 Phrasal Verbs for Solving Problems

These phrasal verbs are commonly used when addressing workplace issues. They’re professional yet conversational, perfect for emails, calls, or team discussions.

Phrasal Verb Meaning Example
deal with To handle or manage a problem We need to deal with the client’s concerns promptly.
sort out To resolve or fix an issue Let’s sort out the scheduling conflict today.
follow up To check or continue communication I’ll follow up with the team tomorrow morning.
figure out To understand or solve something We’re trying to figure out why the system crashed.
put through To connect someone via phone I’ll put you through to the support team.

💡 Tip: Use “sort out” and “figure out” in casual discussions, but avoid overuse in formal emails.

💬 Functional Expressions for Supportive Communication

These expressions help you respond professionally and empathetically when addressing complaints or issues, showing you’re engaged and committed to finding solutions.

Expression When to Use Example
We’re working on it. To show a solution is in progress We’re working on it and will update you soon.
I’ll escalate this. To indicate you’ll pass the issue to higher authority I’ll escalate this to the manager for review.
Let me get back to you. To request time before responding Let me get back to you after checking the details.
Thanks for your patience. To acknowledge someone’s wait Thanks for your patience while we resolve this.

💡 Tip: These phrases work well in customer service or team communication to build trust and clarity.

🧠 How to Use This Vocabulary

Incorporate these expressions into your daily work communication:

  • Emails or phone calls with clients or colleagues
  • Team meetings to discuss and resolve issues
  • Customer service scenarios to address complaints

Practice these phrases in the activities and writing tasks of this session. Pay attention to how they’re used in the “Mistakes at Work” video and try them in your own conversations.

Exercise 1 – Multiple Choice: Phrasal Verbs

Instructions: Select the correct phrasal verb to complete each sentence using the dropdown menu, then click "Check Answers" to see feedback.

  • 1. We need to _____ the complaint before it escalates further.
  • 2. Can you _____ me to the technical support team, please?
  • 3. Let’s _____ the details of the project timeline tomorrow.
  • 4. It’s challenging to _____ why the payment didn’t go through.

💡 Tip: Use “deal with” for handling issues and “figure out” for solving or understanding problems.

Exercise 2 – Match the Expression to the Context

Instructions: Select the correct functional expression for each workplace situation, then click "Check Answers" to see feedback.

Situation Expression Feedback
You’re addressing a client who has been waiting for an update.
You need to involve a supervisor to resolve an issue.
You’re actively addressing a technical glitch reported by a user.
You need more time to provide a detailed response.

💡 Tip: These expressions are great for showing empathy and professionalism in customer-facing roles.

Exercise 3 – Fill in the Blank

Instructions: Select the correct phrasal verb from the dropdown to complete each sentence. One option is extra. Click "Check Answers" to see feedback.
Options: deal with – follow up – figure out – sort out – put through

  • 1. I’ll _____ you to the department handling returns.
  • 2. We need to _____ the budget issues before the next meeting.
  • 3. Can you _____ with the supplier to confirm the order details?
  • 4. It’s important to _____ the root cause of the delay.

💡 Tip: “Sort out” is great for resolving logistical or operational issues, while “put through” is specific to phone connections.
